steps.content: Inheritance lets you create a new class from an existing one. The new child class automatically gets all the methods and attributes of the parent class. You write the shared logic once in the parent, then add or customize only what's different in each child. This is the programming version of 'don't repeat yourself.'
The syntax class Dog(Animal): means Dog inherits from Animal. The child class gets everything the parent has — dog.name and dog.speak() work even though Dog didn't define them.

When you define __init__ in a child class, it overrides the parent's __init__. To keep the parent's setup AND add new attributes, call super().__init__(). It's like saying 'do everything the parent does, then add my own stuff.'

A child class can redefine a method that exists in the parent. This is called method overriding. The child's version runs instead of the parent's. Different child classes can override the same method in different ways — and that's where polymorphism shines.

Polymorphism means 'many forms.' When you call speak() on each animal, Python picks the right version automatically. The loop doesn't care what type each animal is — it just calls speak() and gets the right result. That's polymorphism in action.
Use the built-in isinstance() function to check if an object is an instance of a particular class. It also returns True if the object belongs to a child class — because a Dog IS an Animal.

class Animal: pass
class Dog(Animal): pass
class Cat(Animal): pass

dog = Dog()
print(isinstance(dog, Dog))
print(isinstance(dog, Animal))
print(isinstance(dog, Cat))
